Title: No flight disruptions yet despite soaring jet fuel costs, says Loke
Article:
SHAH ALAM: No flight disruptions have been reported so far, despite mounting cost pressures on Malaysia's aviation sector from surging jet fuel prices, Transport Minister Anthony Loke said today.
Loke said there is no government policy to control air ticket prices, except for limited interventions during festive seasons which involve subsidies.
He added that air travel operates in a free market, with prices determined by supply and demand rather than government-imposed caps.
"So far, there is no disruption. But of course, the logistics and transportation sectors are facing very tough challenges right now.
"The market is driven by demand. If we control prices to the extent that airlines cannot cover their costs, they will not operate flights," he told reporters at a press conference today.
Loke warned that price controls could result in fewer flights, which would ultimately drive fares higher due to reduced supply.
He said airlines are already under pressure from the sharp increase in jet fuel costs, forcing them to manage operations more carefully, including potentially reducing flight frequencies on routes with low passenger load.
He gave assurance that there have been no flight cancellations so far, with airlines continuing to honour existing ticket prices, including during the recent Hari Raya festive period.
"There is an obligation that airlines have maintained. I have been assured since the recent festive season, there were no flight cancellations by our airlines," he said.
However, Loke said airfares are expected to rise moving forward, as airlines have begun imposing fuel surcharges on new ticket purchases to cope with escalating costs.
"Some airlines have informed the government that they are looking at rationalising their flights to ensure efficiency.
"This is a temporary measure for them to weather the storm," he said.
He added the government must balance the interests of all stakeholders, including airlines, passengers and the tourism industry.
"Any decision cannot focus on just one sector. If flights are reduced, tourists cannot come in, hotels will be affected, and the economy will suffer," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Illegal cigarettes hit sundry shop sales
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sundry shops are losing customers to illegal cigarette sellers that remain widely available, according to the Federation of Sundry Goods Merchants Associations of Malaysia.
Its president Hong Chee Meng said the continued presence of illicit cigarettes is placing increasing pressure on legitimate neighbourhood retailers.
"These are real businesses run by real families who contribute to the formal economy. When sales are diverted to illegal sellers, it harms both government revenue and the survival of small retailers.
"What we are increasingly seeing is illegal cigarettes being sold openly by unlicensed retailers and non-members, including shops operated by migrant workers who are not part of our association and who do not follow the same regulatory requirements," he said in a statement.
Legal cigarette prices currently range from about RM12.40 to RM18.40 per pack, while illicit cigarettes are commonly sold for between RM3 and RM8.
Hong said the wide price gap gives illegal sellers a strong advantage.
"When cigarettes are sold far below the government-approved price, it naturally attracts more customers. Many consumers are drawn to these outlets because they know they can get cheaper cigarettes there," he said.
He added that cigarettes remain a key traffic driver for sundry shops.
"When customers come in to buy cigarettes, they usually pick up other items such as drinks, snacks or household goods as well. When that purchase shifts to illegal sellers, the impact goes beyond one product; it affects the entire business," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 3 photographers charged for not possessing identification documents
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Three "freelance photographers" were charged in court today for lacking valid identification documents.
They were among
18 cameramen arrested
near the Petronas Twin Towers over an alleged assault on March 22.
Dang Wangi police chief, Assistant Commissioner Sazalee Adam, said: "The three individuals were charged in court today. They pleaded not guilty and have been remanded at Sungai Buloh Prison," he added.
The three photographers were also found carrying fake MyKad.
"They were taken to the
NRD for fingerprint verification.
Checks found their identities could not be traced, and the MyKad are believed to be fake," he told reporters at Dang Wangi district police headquarters today.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Energy security remains paramount amid escalating geopolitical tensions: Petronas
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Energy security will remain paramount as the global energy system comes under increasing strain from escalating geopolitical tensions in West Asia, Petroliam Nasional Bhd (Petronas) said today.
Its president and group chief executive officer Tan Sri Tengku Muhammad Taufik Tengku Aziz said if the entirely avoidable confrontation in the region unfolds in the worst possible way, many countries would directly feel the absence of energy security, if they have not already begun experiencing its impact.
"In Asean, a region of 700 million people, we have seen in rapid succession the Philippines declaring a state of emergency, Thailand signalling that its oil fuel fund will struggle to cope to cushion price increases, and Vietnam beginning to impose travel limitations."
In Malaysia, while the fuel subsidy remains, the government has temporarily adjusted BUDI95 quota to 200 litres from 300 per month previously.
"(Besides this), governments across Asia are also shifting back to coal to cover LNG shortfalls," he said in his keynote address at the Offshore Technology Conference Asia (OTC Asia) 2026 here today.
Tengku Muhammad Taufik said these developments underscored an uncomfortable dilemma, where efforts to pursue other priorities cannot come at the expense of energy security. .
He said disruptions at critical chokepoints such as the Strait of Hormuz which handles roughly 20 million barrels per day, or nearly one fifth of global oil consumption, as well as a similar share of global LNG trade could trigger compounding consequences across industries, including fertilisers and other petrochemical based products, ultimately impacting end consumers.
Tengku Muhammad Taufik added that rising costs and weakening demand signals could push the global economy towards stagflation and stressed that without a readily available, affordable and resilient energy system, a complete disruption to hydrocarbon flows would bring global activities to a halt.
"We have seen that despite calls for an accelerated energy transition, the fact remains, and it's increasingly undeniable, hydrocarbons will continue to play a central role in energy systems not only of today, but also of tomorrow.
"Oil and gas is still responsible for 80 per cent of global energy supply. Petrochemicals are critical inputs not only for the needs of today, for the manufacturing, but also for the manufacturing of solar PVs, as well as wind turbines," he added.
On another note, Tengku Muhammad Taufik said Petronas is working collaboratively with the local oil and gas services and equipment (OGSE) industry through its Petronas Activity Outlook to uplift the sector.
"I have a message for the local OGSE players: it has never been more urgent to evolve so you can become more competitive and more capable in what is already a highly challenging industry," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police arrest 25 touting photography services at KLCC and Saloma Link Bridge
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police detained 25 individuals, including a Filipino national for allegedly touting photography services to foreign tourists near the Petronas Twin Towers and the Saloma Link Bridge yesterday.
The arrests were made during an operation by the Dang Wangi district police headquarters from 10pm to 1am, following an earlier reported brawl at the location.
Dang Wangi police chief Assistant Commissioner Sazalee Adam said investigations found that the group operated in tourist hotspots such as KLCC and the Saloma Link, targeting visitors with paid photography services.
He said they treated the activity as a source of income despite not having other jobs.
"They move in groups but do not have a leader and operate independently, with each individual approaching customers to offer photography services.
"All those arrested, aged between 16 and 48, comprise 24 locals, including one woman, and one Filipino national," he told reporters at the Dang Wangi police headquarters today.
On March 27, media reported that police had detained 18 individuals following a report that a local man and an Indian tourist were assaulted by a group near KLCC on March 22.
https://www.nst.com.my/news/nation/2026/03/1404656/18-freelance-photographers-nabbed-after-klcc-brawl
Sazalee said investigations also revealed that some individuals from earlier arrests were using suspicious identity cards that did not match their fingerprints.
"Checks with the National Registration Department found that the identity cards did not match the holders' fingerprints, indicating they were not citizens.
"For the latest arrests, further checks are ongoing with the department as we are verifying the authenticity of all suspects' identification documents," he said.
He added that police would continue operations and monitoring in tourist areas to curb such activities.
"Police will continue conducting operations to ensure tourist hotspots remain safe and free from criminal activities.
"The public is urged to channel information to the police if they come across any suspicious activities, including such freelance photographer syndicates," he said.
The case is being investigated under Sections 148 and 324 of the Penal Code, as well as Section 25(1) of the National Registration Regulations.
All suspects have been remanded for three days starting today to assist investigations before the case is referred to the Deputy Public Prosecutor for further action.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt keeps Budi Diesel at RM300 for April
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government will maintain the Budi Madani Diesel (Budi Diesel) cash assistance at RM300 per month for April, as it seeks to cushion the impact of rising global fuel prices on targeted groups.
The Finance Ministry said the additional interim RM100 assistance introduced last month will be continued, keeping total monthly aid for Budi Individu and Budi Agri-Komoditi recipients at RM300.
Payments will be disbursed from April 8, benefiting about 340,000 recipients, with an allocation of around RM102 million.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said the government remains attentive to the pressures faced by the rakyat following the rise in global fuel prices.
"The measure is aimed at ensuring diesel users, particularly individuals and those in the agri-commodity sector, continue to receive support and are shielded from being fully exposed to higher global fuel costs," he said in a statement.
Since its introduction in May 2024, the Budi Diesel programme has disbursed RM1.12 billion in cash assistance, helping to ease cost-of-living pressures.
Anwar, who is also Finance Minister, said the targeted approach ensures aid reaches those who need it most while maintaining fiscal discipline amid ongoing global uncertainties.
Budi Individu provides monthly cash assistance to eligible diesel vehicle owners, while Budi Agri-Komoditi supports small-scale farmers, livestock breeders and smallholders registered with relevant ministries.
Under both categories, the base assistance of RM200 was increased by an interim RM100 from March, bringing the total to RM300 per month.
Eligible applicants must be Malaysian citizens earning RM100,000 annually or below, among other criteria. Payments are credited into registered bank accounts or made in cash at Bank Simpanan Nasional branches for those without bank accounts.
The ministry said it is also considering medium- and long-term measures to ensure the subsidy system remains sustainable, transparent and continues to benefit the rakyat.
"The government continues to closely monitor developments in the global energy market and stands ready to take additional measures swiftly to ensure supply remains stable and sufficient," it said.
Applications for Budi Diesel remain open throughout the year via the official website.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Klang crash raises question: Can victims claim if driver was drunk or on drugs?
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Victims of road accidents involving drunk or drug-impaired drivers in Malaysia are still entitled to compensation, despite widespread misconceptions that such claims are automatically void, legal experts said.
Lawyer Datuk Shanmugam Ganesan told the New Straits Times that Malaysian insurance law is fundamentally designed to protect third-party victims — not errant drivers.
He said under the Road Transport Act 1987, all motorists are required to have at least third-party insurance coverage.
"Many people assume that if a driver is intoxicated, any insurance claim becomes invalid. That is not accurate," he said.
Shanmugam said third-party insurance exists to ensure innocent victims are compensated for injuries, fatalities or property damage, regardless of the at-fault driver's condition at the time of the incident.
Lawyer Vinesh Segar concurred, stressing that the law distinguishes between the rights of victims and the obligations of insurers to their policyholders.
"The driver may have breached the terms of his insurance policy by driving under the influence. That could allow the insurer to deny his own claims or even recover losses from him later," he said.
"However, that does not extinguish the rights of third-party victims."
Vinesh said that under Section 96 of the Act, insurers may still be required to satisfy a judgment obtained by a victim against the at-fault driver, even if policy conditions were breached.
Shanmugam added that, in practical terms, victims can initiate a civil suit against the driver for negligence.
"Once judgment is obtained, the insurer is generally obliged to pay the awarded sum under third-party liability provisions," he said.
Both lawyers noted that while victims can pursue claims directly against the at-fault driver's insurer, the process may become more complex if liability is disputed or if the insurer initially refuses indemnity due to policy breaches.
"In such situations, legal action may be necessary to enforce the claim," Vinesh said.
He also advised that victims may opt to claim under their own insurance policies for quicker repairs, with insurers later seeking recovery from the responsible party through subrogation.
The lawyers further highlighted that in cases where the offending driver is uninsured or holds an invalid policy, victims are not left without recourse.
"They can still seek compensation through the Motor Insurers' Bureau of Malaysia, which provides relief for victims of uninsured or hit-and-run drivers," Vinesh said.
He stressed the importance of public awareness, noting that misinformation often discourages victims from pursuing legitimate claims.
"The system in Malaysia is victim-centric. The wrongdoing of a driver, including intoxication, does not negate the legal protections afforded to innocent parties," he added.
Vinesh also urged accident victims to promptly lodge police reports, gather evidence at the scene and seek legal advice where necessary to safeguard their rights.
"The key message is simple — if you are a victim, you still have the right to claim," he said.
Shanmugam and Vinesh were commenting on a recent case in Klang where a suspected drunk driver crashed into a motorcyclist, resulting in the rider's death.
The victim, Amirul Hafiz Omar, 33, had left home at about 10am on Sunday to go to Section 19 to purchase a Mathematics book for his eldest child when he was involved in a head-on collision with a Honda City.
The 28-year-old driver was headwing towards Klang town from Jalan Raya Barat at high speed. He was believed to have overtaken several vehicles before entering the opposite lane and colliding with Amirul's motorcycle.
The impact caused Amirul to be flung onto a Perodua Myvi. He was pronounced dead at the scene.
The driver, who was remanded for four days, tested positive for both alcohol and drugs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tune Talk targets 5mil subscribers ahead of IPO plans
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Tune Talk Sdn Bhd is targeting five million subscribers before pursuing an initial public offering (IPO), its co-founder and chief executive officer Gurtaj Singh Padda said.
He said the company currently has 1.8 million subscribers and aims to approach the five million mark by end-2027.
"The end of 2027 is where we think we should be closer to five million. That's the effort," he told reporters after Tune Talk's media roundtable here today.
Gurtaj said any decision on an IPO will be made collectively, noting that employees are now shareholders and will have a say in the outcome.
In February, Tune Talk established an Employee Share Trust Scheme to recognise employee contributions and position staff as equity participants.
Under the scheme, Gurtaj transferred 4.15 per cent of Tune Talk's issued share capital into a trust for employees, in accordance with a formal trust deed.
Separately, Tune Talk plans to introduce an e-roaming "roam-as-home" feature, allowing customers to use roaming services at no additional cost under existing plans.
The rollout will begin with Zone 1 covering selected Asian countries, followed by additional zones.
"For Zone 2, we will be in the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, Jordan, Egypt and Oman.
"Then Zone 3, Europe including France, Germany, Spain, Italy, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands, Switzerland, Portugal and Denmark," he said.
Gurtaj said Zone 1 is expected to be activated by May, with Zone 2 to follow within a month, and subsequent zones rolled out thereafter.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Over 10,000 fake royal accounts taken down - Fahmi
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Over 10,000 fake accounts reportedly misusing the identities of His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, Her Majesty Raja Permaisuri Agong Raja Zarith Sofiah, Queen of Malaysia and the royal family for fraud and scams have been taken down.
Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said the figure also includes accounts impersonating members of the Pahang royal family.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: BAT Malaysia to trim workforce ahead of distribution revamp
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: British American Tobacco (Malaysia) Bhd (BAT Malaysia) will undertake a workforce optimisation exercise as part of efforts to improve operational efficiency.
In a filing with Bursa Malaysia, the company said the move comes ahead of the implementation of its new route-to-market distribution model, scheduled to begin on July 1.
"The workforce optimisation is part of the company's operational efficiency initiative and is intended to align its workforce with future operating requirements and business direction," it said.
BAT Malaysia said affected employees will receive their statutory and contractual entitlements, including retrenchment benefits where applicable.
It added that the exercise is not expected to have a material impact on its financial performance for the financial year ending Dec 31, 2026.
At the time of writing, BAT Malaysia's shares were trading at RM5.66, up seven sen or 1.25 per cent, with 285,300 shares done, giving the company a market capitalisation of RM1.6 billion.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NetApp appoints Moti Uttam as Malaysia country manager
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Nasdaq-listed NetApp Inc's unit NetApp Malaysia Sdn Bhd has appointed Moti Uttam as country manager, as the company strengthens its local leadership.
The company said the appointment will support its engagement with Malaysian enterprises as they modernise data infrastructure and adopt artificial intelligence (AI).
Moti has more than 25 years of industry experience, with previous roles at Hitachi Vantara and Dell EMC, and was most recently chief technology and operations officer at Tao Bin Malaysia.
He will focus on expanding customer and partner engagement, as well as driving adoption of the company's data management and AI solutions.
NetApp Asean senior director Angeline Lim said the appointment reflects the company's focus on supporting enterprise adoption of data and AI capabilities.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Loke: 7 Malaysian ships cleared for toll-free passage through Hormuz
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Transport Minister Anthony Loke today confirmed that seven Malaysian-owned vessels currently stranded at the Strait of Hormuz will soon be allowed to pass through toll-free.
Loke said Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim had conveyed the development to the National Economic Action Council (NEAC), following Teheran's commitment to grant passage in view of Malaysia's close diplomatic ties with Iran.
"No — this is a complete no (on Malaysian vessels having to pay toll). The Iranian ambassador (to Malaysia) has mentioned this.
"We are a friendly party and we have good diplomatic relations with the Iranian government. The ambassador has said that no toll is being imposed on Malaysian vessels," he said during a press conference after attending a school funding ceremony for SMKJ Chan Wa here.
Yesterday, in an exclusive interview with the New Straits Times, Iranian Ambassador to Malaysia Valiollah Mohammadi Nasrabadi said Teheran had been informed of the vessels' presence in the Persian Gulf and their intention to transit the strategic waterway, adding that they would be allowed to pass safely.
He also said the vessels would not be subject to any toll charges and would be
allowed to transit the strait free of charge
.
Meanwhile, Loke reiterated the Iranian Embassy's assurance that the vessels would be able to pass safely, although they would need to take turns due to congestion in the area.
"The prime minister has announced that we have obtained clearance and support from the Iranian government to ensure the safe passage of our ships.
"But there are many ships stranded there, anchoring in the area. They have to take turns to pass through the Strait of Hormuz," he added.
On Saturday, Foreign Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan said seven Malaysian-owned tankers were
awaiting clearance
to transit the Strait of Hormuz.
He said the ships were not being detained, but were waiting for a safe window and official clearance to proceed.
Restrictions on vessel movements in the strait came after Iran limited transit through the key global chokepoint in retaliation for large-scale military strikes by the United States and Israel on Feb 28 targeting multiple sites in Iran, which Teheran said included civilian and strategic infrastructure.
The attacks have since triggered a broader regional crisis, with Iran retaliating by imposing restrictions on vessel movements in the Strait of Hormuz.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: King calls for prudent public spending amid global uncertainty
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, has highlighted the importance of prudent public spending to safeguard national resources and ensure they are managed effectively and efficiently.
In a Facebook post today, His Majesty said such an approach was particularly important as the country navigates global economic uncertainty.
The King also highlighted the government's cost-saving measures aimed at strengthening the nation's financial position.
Sultan Ibrahim said this when granting an audience to Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim at Istana Bukit Tunku here.
Also present were National Security Council director-general Datuk Raja Nushirwan Zainal Abidin and Petronas president and group chief executive officer Tan Sri Tengku Muhammad Taufik Tengku Kamadjaja Aziz.
During the meeting, the King was briefed on the latest developments in the economy and national security, including cost of living issues and measures implemented by the government to ensure sustainable economic growth.
The briefing also covered national security matters, such as internal security, border control, and the preparedness of security forces.
The post said the briefing reflected his concern over current national developments.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rawang temple row: Yayasan Kubra wins bid to quash court order over land ownership inquiry
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Yayasan Kubra succeeded in its application at the High Court today to set aside the preliminary order of the Selayang magistrate's court.
The lower court had earlier ruled to proceed with an inquiry into a complaint regarding an unregulated temple in Rawang after hearing issues related to a land dispute and the possibility of a breach of peace.
Judicial Commissioner Suriyati Hasimah Mohd Hashim made the decision after hearing arguments from lawyer Muhammad Hariz Md Yusoff, who represented the foundation as applicant, while lawyer Rajesh Nagarajan, who represented the Sri Uchimalai Muniswaraman Devotees Association, as respondent.
"The court allows the applicant's application and therefore, the decision of the Selayang magistrate's court is set aside.
"If any party is dissatisfied, please file an appeal and the court will provide the grounds of judgment," she said.
On March 13, magistrate Chai Guan Hock ordered both parties involved in the dispute to appear in court for an inquiry under Section 99(4) of the Criminal Procedure Code.
He requested them to file written statements regarding the actual ownership of the disputed land.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia open to China automotive investments, says Johari
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia remains open to automotive investments from China, with its policy framework designed to facilitate high-value participation rather than restrict market entry, the Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry (Miti) said.
Minister Datuk Seri Johari Abdul Ghani said Chinese automotive brands have a significant presence in Malaysia, with 14 of the 34 foreign marques in the market as at December 2025, including BYD, Chery, Jaecoo, Jetour, Haval, Wey, MG and Volvo.
He said BYD Automotive Malaysia Sdn Bhd was granted an interim manufacturing licence on Sept 29, 2025, while Chery Automobile Co Ltd received approval on June 26, 2025 to undertake local vehicle production.
"These approvals demonstrate that our policy framework is not a barrier to entry, but rather a framework for meaningful and high-value participation by foreign investors," he said in a statement.
Johari said Malaysia's automotive policies are developmental in nature, aimed at moving the country towards advanced manufacturing rather than limiting competition.
Under the National Automotive Policy 2020 and the New Industrial Master Plan 2030, he said Malaysia aims to position itself as a regional production and export hub for next-generation vehicles.
Malaysia has more than 592 specialised automotive vendors and a skilled technical workforce capable of supporting world-class electric vehicle (EV) manufacturing, he added.
"Malaysia stands ready to partner manufacturers who come not only to serve the domestic market, but to build for global markets, and our policy framework is designed to enable and accelerate that ambition," he said.
Johari said the country has a long-standing track record as a destination for high-quality foreign automotive investments, supported by established industrial ecosystems such as the Automotive High-Tech Valley in Tanjung Malim.
He said global players including Mercedes-Benz, Volvo and Stellantis have chosen Malaysia as a regional assembly base, while Chery has begun developing its manufacturing facility in Beringin High-Tech Automotive Valley.
Malaysia's central location within Asean provides access to a regional market of more than 600 million consumers, further supported by 17 free trade agreements that open global markets to locally assembled vehicles.
Johari described the country's workforce as technically skilled, English-proficient and experienced in precision manufacturing, while the regulatory environment remains stable, transparent and consistently enforced.
"For manufacturers seeking to build a long-term, export-oriented presence in Southeast Asia, Malaysia remains one of the most attractive destinations in the region.
"Our policies are not intended to close doors, but to ensure that every investment creates deep local value, technology transfer and sustainable employment opportunities," the minister said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Teachers 'relieved' after first SPM cohort without UPSR, PT3 shines
Article:
BANGI: Teachers felt "relieved and grateful" as last year's Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) results were surprisingly strong, marking the first batch without Ujian Pencapaian Sekolah Rendah and Pentaksiran Tingkatan 3.
Computer Science teacher Norzila Oon described an emotional and rewarding day as students received their SPM results, with her department achieving a 100 per cent pass rate.
"Alhamdulillah. My heart was thumping this morning, unsure how the students would perform. When I learned that 37 of 45 candidates earned an A, I felt truly relieved and grateful," she said.
With 17 years of teaching experience, including seven at the school, Norzila said she was deeply moved by her students' achievements, having witnessed their journey firsthand.
UPSR was abolished in 2021 and PT3 discontinued in 2022, both replaced by school-based assessments.
Teacher Suzara Makpol from Sekolah Menengah Kebangsaan Dato Abu Bakar Baginda said the students' outstanding SPM results were especially meaningful given the challenges of the academic year.
"We had very little time to complete the syllabus and revise, and the examination schedule was moved earlier.
"Despite that, the students delivered excellent results, which we are truly grateful for," she said in an interview.
"Students excelled in Pendidikan Syariah Islamiah, Pendidikan Islam and Bahasa Arab, with seven from the religious stream achieving straight A's – the highest scoring 11A and others 9A," she added.
Meanwhile, teacher Azura Deris said the cohort faced a unique challenge, transitioning directly from primary-level assessments to SPM without the usual lower secondary preparation.
"This batch only had Ujian Penilaian Kelas Kafa before going straight to SPM. Previously, students would have PT3 as preparation.
"Without that, it was challenging to prepare them for a major examination, as the syllabus and expectations at upper secondary level are very different," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bank Negara: Fuel subsidy changes unlikely to drive broad inflation
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bank Negara Malaysia expects fuel subsidy rationalisation and supply chain costs to have a limited impact on inflation, without triggering broad-based price increases.
Governor Datuk Seri Abdul Rasheed Ghaffour said Malaysia's inflation outlook of 1.5 per cent to 2.5 per cent reflects expectations that external cost pressures will remain contained, supported by stable domestic demand and moderate cost conditions.
"Government policy measures, including targeted subsidy frameworks, are designed to cushion the impact of global energy and food price shocks, thereby limiting the extent to which higher upstream costs are passed on to consumers," he said.
While acknowledging that energy-related adjustments may raise transportation and logistics costs, Abdul Rasheed said the degree of pass-through depends on how firms respond, with businesses either absorbing part of the increase due to competitive pressures or adjusting prices gradually.
He said any inflationary impact is expected to be sector-specific rather than broad-based, with more pronounced effects in fuel- and transport-intensive industries.
He added that fuel-related cost increases are typically treated as a supply shock, with monetary policy responding based on broader economic conditions rather than mechanically.
"While a supply shock could increase prices and affect domestic conditions, we also examine second-round effects on input prices and consumer prices, depending on producers' willingness to transfer costs to consumers.
"Demand conditions are also important, as higher prices can trigger adjustments in demand," Abdul Rasheed said.
Deputy governor Datuk Marzunisham Omar said the country's inflation position remains favourable, with headline inflation at about 1.4 per cent and producer price inflation recording negative growth in recent months.
He said while higher energy costs typically first affect producer prices before filtering into consumer prices, historical patterns show the pass-through from the Producer Price Index to the Consumer Price Index is usually gradual and limited.
"This moderation is partly due to existing policy safeguards, including targeted diesel subsidies that help contain logistics and transportation costs, as well as regulatory monitoring and enforcement to prevent excessive or opportunistic price increases," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia should leverage Japan ties to win investment, says Nazir
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia should capitalise on its political stability and long-standing ties with Japan to attract foreign investment, particularly as global uncertainties, including the Middle East conflict, continue to disrupt supply chains.
Asean Business Advisory Council Malaysia chairman Tan Sri Nazir Razak said the country has maintained a strong track record in foreign direct investment, remaining an attractive destination despite ongoing global volatility.
"We should double down on our global relationships as Malaysia and Japan share a 70-year diplomatic relationship and a 50-year elevated business partnership," he told reporters after a panel session at the New Japan-Malaysia Industrial Cooperation seminar.
He said the bilateral partnership has weathered multiple crises, adding that current geopolitical disruptions present an opportunity for Malaysia to showcase its stability, policy predictability and investment potential.
"This is yet another opportunity for Malaysia. We need to highlight our strengths, stability, ecosystem and talent to international investors as they reposition their supply chains and investments," he said.
Nazir, who spent 29 years at CIMB Group, including as chief executive officer and chairman, said continuity in governance is key to ensuring policy predictability, adding that political events such as elections should not derail long-term investment strategies.
While acknowledging competition from regional peers such as Vietnam, which offers single-party certainty, a strong labour force and a larger domestic market, he said Malaysia's open economy and policy consistency remain key advantages.
He also called on Malaysian companies to align with Japan's evolving industrial strategy, which focuses on 17 priority sectors, to tap opportunities arising from shifting global economic dynamics.
Japan's ambassador Shikata Noriyuki said Malaysia continues to offer strong investment prospects, although rising costs and a firmer ringgit may require Japanese investors to adopt new business models.
He said both countries face similar challenges in economic and energy security, underscoring the need for closer cooperation to mitigate regional and global supply risks.
Shikata pointed to ongoing collaborations under Asean and with partners such as Australia, including decarbonisation initiatives, while highlighting Malaysia's stability and neutrality as a base for Japanese industrial operations.
He added that Japanese financial institutions and corporations should take a more proactive approach in strategic sectors such as semiconductors, artificial intelligence and data centres to strengthen industrial cooperation.
Meanwhile, University of Tokyo professor Suzuki Kazuto said economic, energy and food security are becoming increasingly interconnected, with supply chain resilience now central to economic security.
"Trust is becoming a value. You cannot do business with countries you cannot trust. The established diplomatic and economic relationship between Japan and Malaysia is therefore important," he said.
Suzuki highlighted Malaysia's role as a natural gas producer and an industrial hub for Japanese companies, adding that a deeper division of labour across Asia could help build a more resilient regional economic network.
While acknowledging that Malaysia and Japan alone cannot directly influence developments in West Asia, he said both countries could still play a role in mitigating risks.
"Malaysia, as a Muslim country, and Japan, which has long-standing friendly relations with Iran, may have avenues to connect and reduce pressure. We should continue exploring ways to mitigate the impact," Suzuki added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Education Ministry to develop long-term plan to face heatwaves
Article:
KAJANG: The Education Ministry will develop a comprehensive long-term plan to face heatwaves to minimise disruptions to the academic calendar and to protect students' health.
Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek said while short-term measures like Home-based Teaching and Learning (PdPR) was an option, a more sustainable strategy was needed to deal with hot weather.
"We get guidance and advice from the Health Ministry and Malaysian Meteorological Department.
"We will work closely with them to ensure our plans are organised and effective, without interfering with the children's learning process."
Fadhlina said the ministry's top priority was the safety and well-being of the school community.
"Our priority is the children's education and also the health of our school citizens. Therefore, all decisions must be made based on guidance from experts at the Health Ministry and MetMalaysia."
The minister was asked to comment on suggestions from experts about improving school infrastructure, including constructing covered areas for co-curricular activities and installing air-conditioning units.
Fadhlina acknowledged that air-conditioning was increasingly being seen as a necessity rather than a luxury.
"Those are all good suggestions. If the guidelines for covered areas in schools can be improved, we will do our best to do so."
She said the ministry was open to expert recommendations to improve the school environment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Miti reviewing CBU floor price, says no ban on new pickups
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry (Miti) is reviewing the minimum price policy for imported vehicles as part of efforts to strengthen local automotive assembly while allowing market forces to play a greater role.
Minister Datuk Seri Johari Abdul Ghani said the current policy sets a minimum on-the-road (OTR) price of RM250,000 for completely built-up (CBU) passenger vehicles under the Approved Permit (AP) framework.
However, a temporary relaxation introduced in 2022 lowered the floor price for imported electric vehicles (EVs) to RM100,000 until Dec 31, 2025 to encourage early adoption.
"Miti is currently reviewing the 'floor price' policy to further strengthen local assembly activities while ensuring that CBU vehicle pricing is driven by market forces," he said in a statement, in response to a news report on conditions imposed on Chinese EV maker BYD.
The minister said the temporary measure was designed to stimulate EV demand while giving national automakers and the broader ecosystem time to develop capabilities and supply chains.
"Local manufacturing and assembly, not perpetual CBU imports, is the government's longer-term strategy for making EVs more accessible and affordable to Malaysians," he said.
Separately, Johari dismissed claims that it had banned the import of new pickup truck models, including those from BYD and Great Wall Motor.
He said there is no restriction on new pickup truck brands entering the Malaysian market, noting that commercial vehicles are subject to localisation requirements, while CBU imports are allowed under limited quotas through the Market Research Pre-Assembly Approved Permit.
"There is no announcement from Miti to ban the importation of new brand pickups into Malaysia," he said.
It was reported that BYD may rethink its plans for a completely knocked down facility in Tanjung Malim due to conditions requiring up to 80 per cent of production to be exported and a minimum pricing threshold.
Johari said the conditions imposed, including export orientation, localisation requirements and pricing thresholds, are applied consistently to all new automotive investments and are not specific to BYD.
He said its broader policy framework is aimed at positioning Malaysia as a regional production and export hub for next-generation vehicles under the National Automotive Policy 2020 and the New Industrial Master Plan 2030.
Johari said the country has more than 592 specialised automotive vendors and a skilled technical workforce to support world-class EV manufacturing, adding that Malaysia remains ready to partner manufacturers not only to serve the domestic market but to build for global markets.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Students turn to YouTube, self-study to ace SPM
Article:
BANGI: Watching YouTube was a key learning tool for some students who sat for the Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) examination as the first batch without taking PT3 and UPSR, with their approach shaped by online learning during the Covid-19 pandemic.
Some students from SMK Dato Abu Bakar Baginda in Sungai Merab, Bandar Baru Bangi, turned to YouTube as a primary learning tool for weaker subjects since Form Two.
"I started learning through YouTube from Form Two. At that time, I was not confident about achieving excellent results, but I kept going," said Nur Hidayah Qistina, who scored 9As.
She said the shift to fully online learning during the Covid-19 pandemic had encouraged students to become more independent and resourceful in their studies.
Meanwhile, Che Wan Fatima Alyana, who scored 7As, said YouTube had been particularly helpful for subjects such as History and Mathematics.
"If I feel confused, I just search on YouTube because it is easier to understand. The way the teachers simplify topics in the videos makes it clearer," she said.
Che said online learning during the pandemic had made digital platforms a key reference point, as they offer greater flexibility compared to live classes.
"In live classes, it can be difficult to ask questions because there are so many students. But on YouTube, we can rewatch and find answers ourselves," she said.
Syifa', who obtained 8As, said although she did not attend paid tuition classes, she made full use of free online learning resources, including recorded lessons and virtual classes.
"I joined free classes on Zoom or watched recorded sessions. I also studied in groups with my friends, especially for Mathematics," she said.
Despite coming from a modest background, she said financial constraints did not stop her from achieving strong results.
"I did not want to burden my parents. I only asked for money when it was really necessary. Most of the time, I relied on my own effort to study," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SPM 2025 success: Multi-level interventions drive best results in 5 years
Article:
KAJANG: Malaysia's best SPM performance in five years has been credited to a series of multi-level interventions, ranging from academic coaching and socio-economic assistance to personal home visits.
Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek attributed the 2025 cohort's outstanding results to a robust "education ecosystem" shaped through ongoing performance dialogues and comprehensive support frameworks.
"Our focus has been on sustained interventions since every result announcement, with performance dialogues designed to continuously enhance support for our students," she told reporters.
"At the level of state education departments, district education offices, and schools, we have implemented an accountability approach for principals to ensure attendance and manage student support systems," she said.
Highlighting that financial hardship remained a key barrier to education, Fadhlina pledged that the MoE would continue providing targeted socio-economic support to keep underprivileged students in school.
"If there are students unable to attend school due to financial constraints, the MoE will step in to assist. In collaboration with the Finance Ministry, we have established programmes such as Tuisyen Rakyat Madani and financial literacy initiatives to emphasise the importance of prioritising education," she said.
She added that these academic interventions were "continuous and consistent," aimed at fostering resilience among students.
One of the most hands-on initiatives is the Ziarah Cakna (Home Visit) programme, where educators personally visit the homes of at-risk students.
"We go as far as visiting homes to show that our teachers are committed and deeply concerned for our children. We want to ensure they remain within the education system," she said.
Fadhlina credited this "strong education ecosystem," supported by teachers across primary and secondary schools, for the success of the 2025 SPM cohort.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Appellate court to decide on federal bid to stay Sabah's 40pct revenue claim
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The Court of Appeal will rule on April 6 on the federal government's bid to stay the execution of Sabah's 40 per cent revenue entitlement.
Sabah Law Society (SLS) president Datuk Mohamed Nazim Maduarin said the Court of Appeal today concluded the hearing, which was conducted via Zoom.
"The panel has fixed April 6, 2026, for delivery of its decision. The application concerns whether the High Court's orders, including the timelines set by the court, are to remain in force pending the disposal of the appeal," he said in a statement.
Mohamed Nazim said the SLS will consider the Court of Appeal's decision once delivered.
"In the meantime, the Society remains guided by the Court's directions," he said.
In February this year, the federal government filed the stay application, naming SLS as the first respondent and the Sabah state government as the second respondent.
In the Notice of Motion, the appellant sought, among other things, that the High Court order dated Oct 17, 2025 (Application for Judicial Review No.: BKI-25-14/6-2022) (as annexed in Annexure A), save and except for paragraph 2(b) thereof, be stayed pending the disposal of the appeal before the Court of Appeal.
The appellant also sought that there be no order as to costs, and such further and/or other relief that the court deems fit and proper to grant.
On Oct 17, last year SLS had succeeded in their judicial review to get an order from a High Court here directed the federal and state governments to review the 40 per cent claim of the net revenue derived from Sabah from 1974 up to 2021.
LINK: https://www.nst.com.my/news/regional/2025/10/1296611/high-court-rules-favour-sls-sabahs-40pct-revenue-entitlement-case
In her ruling, Datuk Celestina Stuel Galid, among others, allowed an order of mandamus sought by SLS to direct the federal government to hold another review with the state government under Article 112D of the Federal Constitution.
This is to give effect to the federation's obligation to make the 40 per cent entitlement payment to the State of Sabah under Article 112C, read with subsection (1) of section 2 of Part IV of the Tenth Schedule of the Federal Constitution, for each consecutive financial year from 1974 to 2021.
The review must be held within 90 days, and an agreement reached within 180 days from the date of the order.
The SLS filed for a judicial review in 2022 and named the government of the federation of Malaysia and the Sabah state government as the first and second respondents respectively in the said judicial review.
SLS, among other things, alleged that the federal government had breached the Federal Constitution by failing to conduct a mandatory revenue review every five years since 1974.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cancer patient Fernandez Minggat makes SMK Tinggi Kajang proud [WATCH]
Article:
KAJANG: For Fernandez Minggat, the journey to the Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) was not marked by classroom lessons and library sessions, but with hospital stays and gruelling cancer treatments.
Diagnosed with cancer and paralysed from the waist down, Fernandez became a symbol of grit for SMK Tinggi Kajang, proving that even the most daunting medical battles cannot extinguish the flame of ambition.
He has been hospitalised many times since December 2022, but Fernandez has achieved a remarkable feat: scoring only As and Bs for all subjects.
He spent the better part of two years studying from a hospital bed. While his peers were in classrooms, he was navigating his illness, often forced to self-study while undergoing treatment.
"I've been hospitalised too many times to count," he said, reflecting on the long months of recovery.
"But I never gave up. I took the extra effort to study even when I was hospitalised because I knew I wanted a future."
His perseverance has put him on the path to achieving his dream of becoming an interior designer. He plans to pursue his studies at Universiti Teknologi Mara, crediting his survival and success to his faith and his family.
"Without them, I don't know if I would be here. My message to others is this: even if you are a PWD (person with disabilities), do not let that be the reason that stops you from your success."
Fernandez's story of resilience was the crown jewel of a day of celebration for SMK Tinggi Kajang, where a culture of collective commitment saw a wave of students achieving excellent results.
Principal Fareed Faheem Mohd Elyas, who began leading the school last October, said the school's SPM success was the result of intense dedication from both staff and students.
"We had extra classes starting as early as 7.10am. We even had classes during the holidays," Fareed said.
"The most heartening thing was that the teachers weren't forced to come, they wanted to be here. The students shared that same hunger."
Top scorer Kaartic Mahendran, 18, said while the academic rigour was high, the emotional support from teachers made the difference.
"The teachers were a great support, morally and emotionally. When we were down, they were the first to notice and be there for us. They are experts in their fields and guided us every step of the way," said Kaartic, who achieved a perfect 11As.
For district-level footballer Arif Daniel, who scored straight As, his SPM journey was about overcoming his weakest subject, Mathematics.
"I failed once with a score of 21," he said.
Driven by his parents' emphasis on education, he transformed that failing grade into an 'A', while maintaining his commitment to football.
"My family was my biggest motivation. Putting them first was the mindset that pushed me to overcome my challenges."
As the cheers subsided at SMK Tinggi Kajang, the legacy of the 2025 cohort remains clear: whether battling life-threatening illness or academic failure, the spirit of "High Kajang" is the refusal to give up.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 9A SPM student inspired by parents' hard work
Article:
IPOH: Determined to secure a brighter future for her family, Wan Aleya Batrisyia Meor Roslan achieved an impressive 9A in the Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) examination.
The Sekolah Menengah Kebangsaan Raja Permaisuri Bainun student described the result as a delightful surprise, marking the fulfilment of her long-held goal of making her parents proud.
"I didn't think I could achieve this, but I'm very grateful. I just wanted to make my parents proud," she said when met at the school today.
Wan Aleya is one of 13 students at the school to score straight As.
Growing up in a modest household, she said watching her parents' tireless work in a factory shaped her determination from a young age.
"My parents are not well-off, but my mother raised me to be strong. Seeing how hard she works makes me want to succeed and improve our family's future. They are one of the main reasons I strive to do my best," she said.
Rather than spending long hours studying, Wan Aleya, who is also an accomplished violinist, prioritised consistency and understanding.
"I don't study for too long because I might get sleepy. I usually spend about three hours, early in the morning and before going to bed, revising what I've learned," she said.
The eldest of three siblings tailored her approach according to the subject, favouring discussion over memorisation for theory-heavy topics.
"For History, I like to tell stories with my friends because it helps me remember better. But for Mathematics, there's no choice – you have to keep practising. I also recited Al-Ma'thurat every morning before SPM. It helped me stay calm and gave me confidence when answering questions," she added.
Despite her excellent results, Wan Aleya acknowledged that financial constraints remain a concern as she plans her next step.
"I applied to Universiti Teknologi Petronas, but I am worried I might not get in. I was also concerned that my parents might not be able to afford the costs. I hesitated to complete my application because I feared my mother would not have the financial means to support my studies. I am planning to enrol in a foundation programme and am interested in pursuing engineering," she said.
Wan Aleya, 18, who aspires to become a lecturer, said she hoped to one day repay her parents and buy them a home.
"My parents have been renting a house in Meru Raya for 19 years and cannot afford to buy one. It has always been my dream to buy a home for them one day," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SPM 2025: Student with spinal bifida scores 10As
Article:
ALOR SETAR: Despite the challenges of spinal bifida, Huda Rasheeda Rosli, a student at Sekolah Menengah Agama (SMA) Darusaadah here, has demonstrated extraordinary determination, achieving 10As in the 2025 Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM).
She is one of three special educational needs candidates in Kedah to achieve outstanding results, alongside Yousef Mohd Hazril Munir from Sekolah Menengah Kebangsaan (SMK) Khir Johari and Nur Zahra Arifah Abd Rahman from SMA Islahiah Mahang.
"I honestly didn't expect this. My goal was just seven or eight As, so getting 10As feels like a blessing…especially after everything I had to overcome, including being transferred to another school to complete my exam because of the flood.
"After finishing the Bahasa Melayu paper, our teachers told us we had to relocate to another school due to the heavy rain and flooded roads. That was the toughest challenge for me, as I had never stayed in a hostel before," she told reporters today.
Huda Rasheeda said she had to stay in the school hostel for several days before being allowed to return home and continue her examinations at her original school once the floodwaters receded.
"My mother and two siblings have been a constant source of support, and their prayers have kept me going since my father passed away in 2023. I plan to continue with the Sijil Tinggi Agama Malaysia (STAM) at the same school," she said.
Meanwhile, her mother, Atifah Musa, 55, a teacher at a school in Sungai Korok, Jitra, said she knew how difficult it could be for her daughter, who copes not only with a spinal condition but also with anxiety and the challenges of crowded spaces.
"When she had to move into the hostel because of the flood, I was worried she would not be able to focus or study in groups. We are grateful she managed to achieve excellent results," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Palestinians continue to struggle for justice
Article:
ON  the 50th anniversary of Palestinian Land Day (March 30, 2026), we stand united in resistance.  Since 1976, Israel has occupied our land (West Bank, Gaza and East Jerusalem).
Decades of dispossession and expansion have only deepened our resolve: from the Galilee to Gaza, Palestinians are one people with an indivisible homeland. Land Day coincides with the epic "Al-Aqsa Flood" uprising, proving that our will cannot be broken by siege or massacre.
In  Gaza, Israel has killed over 70,000 Palestinians since Oct 7, 2023. The dead have overwhelmingly been women, children and the elderly, and the siege has rendered Gaza uninhabitable.
Almost 90 per cent of Gaza's population is now internally displaced, facing famine under a cruel blockade. On this Land Day, we reaffirm that our right to land, freedom and return is inalienable and eternal.
Palestinian lands conquered in the 1967 war, including East Jerusalem and all of historic Palestine, belong to their indigenous people. Israel's settlements, checkpoints and walls are illegal under international law. This unity of blood and destiny, from Galilee to Gaza, encompasses refugee camps to occupied towns.
The International  Court of Justice has declared Israel's presence in the Occupied Palestinian Territory unlawful (July 2024), and the UN General Assembly (in September 2024) had demanded that Israel end its occupation.
All Israeli land grabs (East Jerusalem annexation, West Bank settlements, Gaza blockade) must cease. We call on the international community to enforce the UN resolutions and restore Palestine's 1976 borders.
We call for justice for the Gaza genocide. Gaza is under siege and systematic attack.  Over 70,000 Gazans have been killed, and tens of thousands more wounded, as Israel bombs homes, hospitals and UN shelters.
The justice for the blood of our martyrs and the sacrifices of the wounded and prisoners remains incomplete without the prosecution of the killers. From the sacred soil of Palestine, we launch a global human rights appeal:
• Issue International Arrest Warrants: We call upon the International Criminal Court  (ICC) to issue immediate arrest warrants for the occupation's political and military leaders as "war criminals against humanity".
• End the Era of Impunity: We urge all free nations to activate their universal jurisdiction to prosecute those who committed these massacres and ensure they do not escape justice.
• Classification of Genocide: We demand explicit international recognition that what is occurring in Gaza is a full-fledged crime of genocide according to international conventions.
We reject Israeli impunity and inequality. A bill to impose the death penalty on  Palestinians is the latest proof of Israel's contempt for human rights. Such laws remove any pretence of justice. We denounce these measures and reiterate that no one  – prime minister, defence minister or soldier – is above international law.
We also reject the double standard that allows Israel to flout the Geneva Conventions (targeting civilians, starving populations, etc) while Palestinians are labelled "terrorists" for resisting.
Resistance is a legitimate right. Under the UN Charter and Fourth Geneva Convention, people under foreign occupation have the right to resist. Any armed liberation struggle is just when civilian oppression is relentless.
Our people will continue to resist the occupation until freedom comes. We reject any attempt to delegitimise our struggle or to barter away Palestinian rights.
We demand liberation and return. Land Day is a covenant of loyalty to our land. No walls, no expulsions and no killings will erase our presence.
CENTRE FOR HUMAN RIGHTS RESEARCH AND ADVOCACY;
GLOBAL LEGAL COALITION FOR SUPPORT OF AL-QUDS AND PALESTINE, MALAYSIA ;
INTERNATIONAL JUSTICE FORUM AGAINST GENOCIDE
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fadhlina: First batch without UPSR, PT3 excels in 2025 SPM
Article:
KAJANG: Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek said the 2025 Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) cohort, the first to sit without Ujian Pencapaian Sekolah Rendah (UPSR) and Pentaksiran Tingkatan 3 (PT3), has shown strong resilience.
"This cohort is often noted for not having sat previous examinations, but they have proven to be resilient," she said.
"They have faced challenges, including the Covid-19 pandemic, and shown remarkable endurance," she added.
Fadhlina's remarks come as the Education Ministry announced the 2025 SPM results, which showed an improvement, with the national average grade (GPK) rising to 4.42 from 4.49 in 2024.
She said the batch's performance contributed to SPM 2025 recording its best results in five years.
"This is the best performance in five years, reflecting the strength of our interventions and the efforts of all involved," she said.
Fadhlina added that ongoing intervention programmes, continuous performance reviews, and support from teachers and parents had driven the success.
Although non-attendance had declined compared to earlier in the year, some students were still absent for the examination, she said.
Fadhlina said the
absenteeism
rate fell to 1.42 per cent in 2025 from 1.81 per cent in the previous year.
UPSR was abolished in 2021 and PT3 discontinued in 2022, both replaced by school-based assessments.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Clerk's son tops SPM with 10A+ as Penang maintains steady performance
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Coming from a humble background did not stop Sanjay Muniandy from excelling in the Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia (SPM) 2025 examination, achieving an outstanding 10A+.
The 18-year-old, whose father works as a clerk at Universiti Sains Malaysia (USM) and whose mother is a housewife, demonstrated that discipline, consistency and determination can overcome limitations.
Sanjay, a Pure Science stream student who also took Principles of Accounting, said his success was rooted in constant practice and paying close attention in class.
"The key is to keep practising. You cannot stop. All exercises given by teachers must be completed, and you must focus during lessons," he said after collecting his results at SMK Bukit Jambul today.
Sanjay is the top student at the school, which is also among the best performing in the state.
To strengthen his understanding, he attended tuition classes for six subjects, including Science, Additional Mathematics and Accounting.
He admitted, however, that the journey was not without challenges.
"I was worried about Moral, Physics and Additional Mathematics, but I kept practising and followed the tips given by my teachers," he added.
An only child, Sanjay typically studied for one to two hours daily and preferred self-study, occasionally collaborating with friends to exchange questions and answers.
He has applied to pursue the matriculation programme and is considering a future in Computer Science.
Meanwhile, fellow high achiever Ahmad "Aqeel" Ibrahim, 17, who scored 8As in all eight subjects he took, credited his success to discipline, teacher support and strong family encouragement.
The Accounts stream student, who obtained A+ in Additional Mathematics, Principles of Accounting, English and Mathematics, said attending extra classes at school was crucial.
"The most important thing is to attend extra classes, build good relationships with teachers, and, of course, prayers from my parents," he said.
Ahmad also stood out for having skipped a year early in his schooling, making him younger than his classmates.
His turning point came after the trial examinations, where he initially obtained only three As.
"I worked harder after the trials. My Additional Mathematics improved from a C, and I managed to raise my overall results," he said.
He now hopes to pursue Mathematics at a higher level and ultimately earn a doctorate in the field.
Their achievements come as Penang maintained its SPM performance, recording a State Average Grade (GPN) of 4.39, unchanged from the previous year.
State Education Department director Mohamad Dziauddin Mat Saad said the consistency reflected candidates' resilience despite challenges throughout the year.
A total of 16,545 out of 17,933 candidates from Education Ministry schools who sat for the examination qualified for certificates, representing 92.26 per cent.
"Although this is a slight decrease of 1.41 percentage points from 93.67 per cent in 2024, the overall number of candidates qualifying for certificates has increased," he said during a press conference announcing the results.
He added that 25 candidates obtained straight A+, compared with 19 in the previous year. For candidates achieving all As (A+, A and A-), 725 were recorded, a slight drop from 800 candidates in 2024.
Dziauddin said 30 schools in the state maintained a 100 per cent pass rate, consistent with last year.
On subject performance, 41 out of 66 subjects showed improvement, while 24 declined and one remained unchanged.
"Islamic Studies recorded the highest improvement of 0.73 points, while Bahasa Melayu saw a decline of 0.28 points," he said, adding that the performance gap between urban and rural schools widened slightly to 1.33 points from 1.25 previously.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man escapes noose, gets 35 years, 12 strokes for killing neighbour
Article:
KLANG: A former lorry driver was sentenced to 35 years in prison and 12 strokes of the rotan after being found guilty of murdering his neighbour with a parang during a dispute in 2022.
Judge Norliza Othman also sentenced S. Panirselvam, 42, to serve 15 years for seriously injuring a woman in the same incident.
The judge ordered both sentences to run concurrently from his arrest on Dec 13, 2022, at Kajang Prison.
In her judgment, Norliza found him guilty on both charges, ruling that he had failed to establish his defence of provocation or lack of awareness that his actions were unlawful.
"The court finds that the prosecution has proven beyond reasonable doubt that you committed offences under Section 302 and 326 of the Penal Code.
"The court also finds that, although you were called to enter defence, you failed to raise any reasonable doubt to rebut the prosecution's case," she said.
Panirselvam was charged with murdering Muhammad Hafiz Abdullah, 52, between 10.30am and 11am in front of a house in Lintang Sungai 8, off Jalan Tepi Sungai on Dec 13, 2022.
The charge under Section 302 carries the death penalty or a jail term between 30 and 40 years upon conviction.
If the death sentence is not imposed, the offender must be whipped not less than 12 times.
He was also charged under Section 326 with causing grievous hurt to Ng Hui Can, 46, at the same time, place and date.
The offence carries a maximum 20-year jail term, a fine or whipping upon conviction.
He surrendered himself to the police after the attack.
Panirselvam, dressed in white prison uniform, appeared composed in the dock as the sentence was delivered, after earlier pleading for a lighter sentence.
In mitigation, defence lawyer Zamri Ibrahim urged the court to spare his client from the death penalty, arguing that a custodial sentence would be more appropriate.
"He regrets his actions and acted out of dissatisfaction with the deceased. His actions were driven solely by that," he said.
Zamri said Panirselvam's circumstances had led to the breakdown of his marriage.
"My client divorced his wife. He understands that he will not be freed from prison and they mutually agreed to separate," he said.
Deputy public prosecutor Farah Aqilah Ahmad Fuad urged the court to impose the death penalty, citing the severity of the offence.
She said that both victims were the accused's neighbours, adding that closed circuit television footage showed the brutality of the attack.
"The recordings show the accused repeatedly slashing both victims without mercy.
"Even when the deceased was no longer able to defend himself, the accused continued the attack.
"This aggravates the severity of the offence and cannot be taken lightly," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Keeping engines running: Govt says Budi Diesel aid to continue amid volatile fuel market
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 31 — The Anwar government will maintain the Budi Diesel aid at RM300 per month to help Malaysians cope with rising global fuel prices.
The Finance Ministry announced today that an additional interim RM100 will continue, keeping total support for individuals and recipients under the Budi Agri‑Commodity scheme at RM300 each.
“This step is being implemented by the government to ensure that diesel users, especially individuals and the agri-commodity sector, continue to receive support and are protected from being fully exposed to global fuel price increases,” the ministry said in a press statement.
The aid, budgeted at  RM102 million, will be distributed starting April 8 and is expected to benefit roughly 340,000 people.
The government said the targeted approach ensures that the support reaches those who need it most, while safeguarding national finances.
In addition to this interim measure, the government is considering medium- and long-term steps to ensure the subsidy system remains sustainable, transparent, and continues to benefit Malaysians.
Officials added that market developments will be closely monitored, and further measures will be taken if necessary to ensure energy supply remains stable and sufficient.
Enforcement has also been strengthened to prevent abuse and ensure subsidies reach eligible recipients.
Applications for Budi Diesel remain open throughout the year via the official website at
https://budidiesel.gov.my
.
Since its launch in May 2024, Budi Diesel has disbursed RM1.12 billion in cash aid, easing the cost of living for targeted groups while maintaining the country’s fiscal discipline amid ongoing global energy pressures.

Title: Malaysia resilient as MTEN studies global energy crisis, Anwar vows proactive measures, welfare focus
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 31 — The National Economic Action Council (MTEN) today conducted an in depth review of the escalating global energy crisis and its implications for Malaysia’s economy and the well being of the people.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said the world is entering a more challenging and uncertain phase, and Malaysia is not immune to its effects.
“We cannot afford to be complacent or overly comfortable. We must remain disciplined, resilient and united,” he said in a Facebook post.
Acknowledging the scale of the challenge, Anwar emphasised that Malaysia’s economic fundamentals remain strong and resilient.
He said the Madani government will continue to act proactively, guided by data and current realities, while also engaging industry players and small and medium enterprises (SMEs) to ensure that every measure taken is comprehensive, balanced and effective.
At the same time, he urged Malaysians to strengthen the domestic economy by prioritising local products and supporting home grown entrepreneurs.
“Our internal strength is the main shield in facing the global energy crisis triggered by the conflict in West Asia,” he said.
Anwar added that every decision will remain anchored in responsibility and the interests of the people.
“The government will strive to safeguard energy supply stability, ensure the economy continues to function effectively, and keep the welfare of the people at the forefront of all actions,” he said.

Title: Six injured in Kajang-Semenyih crash, police nab SUV driver for alleged drink-driving
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 31 — Police have arrested a man suspected of driving under the influence of alcohol after his vehicle was involved in an accident that injured six individuals, including children, on the Kajang-Semenyih Bypass Road, early this morning.
Kajang district police chief ACP Naazron Abdul Yusof said the accident occurred at about 1.20 am involving a Range Rover Velar sports utility vehicle (SUV) driven by a 51-year-old man and a Mazda van.
“The accident occurred when an SUV on the Kajang - Semenyih Bypass suddenly lost control and hit the back of a van, causing the vehicle to overturn onto the left side of the road.
“As a result of the accident, the van driver and five passengers aged seven to 51 suffered injuries and are being treated at the Kajang Hospital and Serdang Hospital while the SUV driver was not injured,” he said in a statement today.
Naazron said the SUV driver was remanded for two days until tomorrow and the case is being investigated under Section 44(1A) of the Penal Code 1987.
He said members of the public with information regarding the incident can contact investigating officer Sergeant Nurul Shaffika Mohd Shair at 013-2091766. — Bernama

Title: No disruptions yet, but aviation and shipping sectors under strain, says transport minister
Article:
SHAH ALAM, March 31 — Transport Minister Anthony Loke said there have been no disruptions to Malaysia’s aviation and logistics sectors so far, although both industries are facing mounting pressure from rising costs due to the ongoing US–Iran tensions, which have driven up global oil and jet fuel prices.
He said the government is closely monitoring the situation and has been engaging regularly with key industry players, particularly airlines, as challenges intensify.
“Airlines are experiencing a significant increase in fuel costs, especially jet fuel.
“While supply remains stable, they must manage costs carefully to avoid heavy losses. Some airlines have informed the government that they are considering rationalising their flight operations,” he said at a press conference during a mock cheque presentation where Tan Sri Lim Wee Chai and Datuk Seri Robin Tan jointly donate RM1 million to SMJK Chan Wa here today.
This, he said, includes airlines looking at the possibility of consolidating routes or reducing flight frequencies, particularly on sectors with low passenger load factors.
He said the measures are temporary and intended to help the industry navigate the current challenges.
The minister also said that similar challenges are affecting the shipping sector, where operators are also grappling with rising operational costs.
“The shipping sector is facing similar challenges. There will likely be surcharges passed on to customers.
“We are looking into ways to ensure these surcharges are implemented in a more systematic and standardised manner,” he added.
Loke said the matters have already been raised at the national level, with shipping players presenting their views to the National Economic Action Council (NEAC).

Title: Loke: Iranian government assures safe passage, no tolls for Malaysian ships in Strait of Hormuz
Article:
KLANG, March 31 — Transport Minister Anthony Loke said Malaysia has received assurances from Iran that its vessels will be able to pass safely through the Strait of Hormuz, following concerns raised over recent developments in the region.
He also clarified that Malaysian vessels will not be subject to any tolls when passing through the strait.
“The Iranian ambassador has confirmed that no toll will be imposed on Malaysian vessels, and it is because we are a friendly party.
“We have a good diplomatic relationship with the Iranian government,” he said at a press conference during a mock cheque presentation where Tan Sri Lim Wee Chai and Datuk Seri Robin Tan jointly donated RM1 million to SMJK Chan Wa here today.
He then said that the matter had also been reported to the National Economic Action Council.
Loke added that while some Malaysian ships are currently anchored and waiting their turn to transit the strategic waterway, the situation remains under control.
On Saturday,
The Star
reported that the seven Malaysian vessels currently held up in the Strait of Hormuz are expected to resume their journey soon after Malaysia’s diplomatic efforts with Iran.
Foreign Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan said Tehran has agreed to allow the ships to pass through the strait, with a designated window for their departure likely to open in the near term.

Title: E‑wallet operator TNG Digital introduces employee fuel subsidy scheme from April 1
Article:
PETALING JAYA, March 31 — TNG Digital Sdn Bhd will roll out an additional RON95 petrol subsidy for its employees from April 1, supplementing the Budi95 scheme once their allocated quota is used up.
The e-wallet operator said it would be the first private employer in Malaysia to introduce such a measure, aimed at cushioning workers from fluctuations in fuel prices,
Free Malaysia Today
(FMT) reported.
“Our responsibility as a company goes beyond building products for millions of Malaysians. It extends to taking care of the people who build those products every day,” said TNG Digital CEO Alan Ni in a statement.
The company said there would be no fixed quota under the initiative, which will cover all employees across its subsidiaries, including contract staff and interns.
It added that the scheme will be integrated into the Touch ‘n Go e-wallet application via the Budi95 feature.
Starting tomorrow, the government will reduce the monthly Budi95 quota from 300 litres to 200 litres in response to the Middle East conflict.

Title: BYD not singled out, Miti says CKD rules designed to protect vendor ecosystem and boost exports
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 31 — The Ministry of Investment, Trade and Industry (Miti) said it imposes no isolated unfavourable conditions on carmakers, stressing that the requirements reflect a consistent policy applied to all new automotive investments in Malaysia since September 2025.
This follows reports that Chinese electric vehicle (EV) manufacturer BYD is re-evaluating its plans for a local completely knocked down (CKD) assembly plant in Tanjung Malim, Perak, claiming that the government has imposed unfavourable conditions for the carmaker.
Its minister, Datuk Seri Johari Abdul Ghani, said the attached conditions are non-discriminatory and are applicable equally to all high-volume automotive assembly projects regardless of brands and countries of origin.
“This is designed to ensure local assembly capacity moves toward sustainable, higher-value market segments and to avoid displacement of the established vendor ecosystem,” he said in a statement today.
The ministry emphasised that BYD is not singled out; the same condition applies to any new CKD entrant.
On the allegation that Miti imposed a condition that 80 per cent of BYD plant’s production must be exported, the ministry responded that the limitation in domestic sales to 10,000 units per year corresponds to 20 per cent of BYD’s total projected production capacity.
“This is a pro-export condition aimed at ensuring that investment contributes to Malaysia’s trade balance and global supply chain integration. It is not a restriction on total production but a strategic measure to encourage export orientation,” said Johari.
He also refuted the allegation that it imposed a condition that only 20 per cent of BYD’s production can be sold locally, and these vehicles must be priced above RM200,000.
“The allegation is inaccurate. The condition imposed for the domestic market is a minimum on the road (OTR) price of RM100,000 for BYD’s CKD vehicles sold domestically.
“This ensures local assembly focuses on higher-value segments, preserving market space for national players like Proton and Perodua while still offering affordable EV solutions,” said Johari.
